history: The client has a history of a past labor dispute at a previous workplace, which appears to have left a lasting impact on their perception of fairness and authority. They continue to experience ongoing concerns related to fairness, pay, and authority at work. This background suggests a pattern of heightened sensitivity to issues of justice and equity, as well as a tendency to ruminate on past disputes and current workplace dynamics.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
I am bound to be rejected
intermediate_belief: If I encounter unfairness or inadequate responses from authority figures, then it means I am not respected or valued, and I must remain vigilant to protect myself from being taken advantage of. I need to be on guard because authority figures often overlook or dismiss my concerns. If I don't advocate for fairness, no one else will.
intermediate_belief_depression: If authority figures continue to ignore or dismiss my concerns, it proves that nothing will ever change for me at work, and I will always be stuck in unfair situations.
coping_strategies: The client tends to ruminate on past disputes and current concerns, often replaying situations in their mind. They may avoid direct confrontation with authority figures or procrastinate on tasks involving them. At times, they seek reassurance from colleagues or become more assertive, which can lead to strained relationships.
situation: Reflecting on a work-related case and the response of a businessman involved.
auto_thought: He doesn't really care about fairness; my concerns are just being ignored again. This always happens when I try to raise issues with people in power.
behavior: I replay the situation in my mind, analyzing what happened and what I could have said. I avoid reaching out to the businessman or authority figure again. I talk to a colleague to seek validation that my concerns are justified.
